--- Page 1 ---
1
Automated Heuristic Design for Network Operations
Reza Namvar, Student Member, IEEE, Jos´e Gallego, Student Member, IEEE, Jose A. Ayala-Romero, Member,
IEEE, Livia Elena Chatzieleftheriou, Member, IEEE, Andres Garcia-Saavedra, Senior Member, IEEE, Albert
Banchs, Senior Member, IEEE, and Marco Fiore, Senior Member, IEEE
Abstract—Network operation relies on heuristics to solve many
tasks rapidly and efficiently across the protocol stack. These
heuristics are the result of thorough human-driven design rooted
in expert knowledge of the target system and problem. Recently,
approaches powered by Artificial Intelligence have shown promis-
ing results in devising solutions that outperform long-established
heuristics in classical problems. We explore the possibility of
applying such Automated Heuristic Design (AHD) frameworks
to network environments by (i) discussing the general integration
of AHD with network operation and the associated challenges,
as well as (ii) proposing a practical implementation of AHD for
a specific networking task, i.e., 5G decoding. Initial results show
how modern AHD tools can devise heuristics for Low-Density
Parity Check decoding on par with state-of-the-art solutions
implemented in production systems.
Index Terms—Automatic Heuristic Design, network operation,
heuristic algorithms, Large Language Models, LDPC decoding.
I. INTRODUCTION
Heuristic algorithms, i.e., fast, solutions that lack formal
performance guarantees but effectively solve NP-hard opti-
mization tasks, are the cornerstone of industry-grade network
operation and provide practical solutions for signal detection,
channel estimation, scheduling, resource allocation, routing, or
congestion control. Traditionally, heuristics are the result of
thoughtful manual design driven by deep domain knowledge,
leading to a combination of computational efficiency and en-
gineering accountability that are hard to beat. In fact, Artificial
Intelligence (AI) models proposed during the past decade to
address the same tasks, e.g., [1]–[6], have largely failed to
supplant long-established heuristics in production networks
due to higher complexity and lack of transparency.
Automated Heuristic Design. Automated Heuristic Design
(AHD) is a different paradigm through which AI can move
beyond conventional human-designed heuristics for networked
systems. Unlike legacy applications of AI that employ complex
computational models trained on massive data to solve the
target task, AHD leverages AI to design a simple heuristic
for the task—thus retaining the efficiency and accountability
properties inherent to heuristic algorithms, while benefiting
from the unparalleled proficiency of AI in devising solutions
with higher performance than human-defined programs.
R. Namvar, J. Gallego, and A. Banchs are with IMDEA Networks Insti-
tute, Madrid, Spain and Universidad Carlos III de Madrid, Spain. E-mail:
{reza.namvar, jose.gallego, albert.banchs}@networks.imdea.org
J. A. Ayala Romero, and A. Garcia-Saavedra are with NEC Lab-
oratories Europe, Heidelberg, Germany. E-mail: {andres.garcia.saavedra,
jose.ayala}@neclab.eu
L. E. Chatzieleftheriou is with the Technical University of Delft, Delft, the
Netherlands. E-mail: L.E.Chatzieleftheriou@tudelft.nl
M. Fiore is with IMDEA Networks Institute, Madrid, Spain. E-mail:
marco.fiore@networks.imdea.org
Originally based on Genetic Programming (GP), AHD has
been revolutionized by the advent of Large Language Models
(LLMs), which have replaced the rigid search spaces and
limited expressiveness of GP with flexible, natural language-
driven code generation. Specifically, recent frameworks have
successfully integrated LLMs into evolutionary loops that
allow refining candidate heuristics while avoiding halluci-
nation or suboptimal code generation. Seminal frameworks
such as FunSearch [7] demonstrated that pairing LLMs with
programmatic evaluators in distributed, island-based architec-
tures and best-shot prompting can lead to novel mathematical
knowledge. Subsequent proposals like Evolution of Heuris-
tics (EoH) [8] and Reflective Evolution (ReEvo) [9] have
enhanced AHD through (i) tailored prompting strategies that
control the trade-off between exploration and exploitation,
(ii) dual-evolution that co-evolves the algorithmic code and
its corresponding natural language explanation, or (iii) verbal
gradients that emulate human expert reflection to provide
natural-language feedback across code generations.
Modern AHD frameworks based on LLMs prove capable of
devising new heuristics that are human-interpretable and attain
significantly better performance than state-of-the-art solutions
designed by domain experts for classical problems like cap
set [7], bin packing [7], traveling salesman [8], flow-shop
scheduling [8], or circle packing [10].
AHD for networking. Motivated by the success of the
paradigm in the context of optimization tasks, we discuss in
this paper the application of LLM-based AHD to network
systems. In fact, using AHD frameworks to solve networking
tasks is not straightforward and requires addressing novel
shortcomings. First, AHD has been validated almost exclu-
sively on static combinatorial optimization problems where
objectives are well defined, evaluations of the solution quality
are inexpensive, and thousands of runs per candidate are
feasible; this is not the case in network systems where met-
rics are multi-dimensional and stochastic, and performance
needs to be assessed via computationally-heavy simulation
or time-consuming experiments. Second, existing AHD tools
are often over-specialized to their target problem structures,
lacking versatility across the wide range of heterogeneous
tasks encountered in networks. Third, public implementations
of state-of-the-art AHD frameworks are often incomplete,
only providing high-level operation pipelines while lacking
detailed file structures and practical specifications required for
deployment in complex networking environments.
Ultimately, directly using existing FunSearch-, EoH-, or
ReEvo-style pipelines to generate an heuristic for a network
task is not possible. Instead, the overall AHD methodology
must be revisited, from how heuristics are embedded into the
arXiv:2605.28197v1  [cs.NI]  27 May 2026


--- Page 2 ---
2
Fig. 1: Overview of AHD applied to network systems, with high-level logical blocks and their implementations.
system to how scores are constructed and how evaluations are
scheduled under strict resource constraints.
Our contributions. We make a first effort to overcome the
challenges above and adapt LLM-based AHD to networking
scenarios. Our contributions are summarized as follows.
• We propose a complete four-phase methodology to tailor
AHD for tasks emerging in the networking domain.
• We present one practical application case study, adapting
an AHD engine to solve a specific sub-function of Low-
Density Parity Check (LDPC) processing, showing results
comparable to state-of-the-art human-designed heuristics.
• We open source our full implementation of a AHD tool
for networking, fostering future research on the topic.
II. HEURISTIC GENERATION FOR NETWORKS
The proposed operation of AHD for network systems is
illustrated in Fig. 1. The AHD engine leverages modern LLMs
to produce heuristics for a specific networking tasks in step
1 . The heuristics are then deployed in the relevant network
environment in step
2
and tested for compatibility with
system requirements and general performance, returning a
score to the AHD engine in step 3 . Best-performing heuristics
are stored in a database to track their evolution and retain the
final code in step 4 .
Implementing the general flow above requires a dedicated
methodology to address four specific design phases, which we
denote as A–D in Fig. 1 and discuss next.
A. AHD Suitability Analysis
Given a target networking problem, the first phase consists
in assessing if the task satisfies the criteria that would make
AHD an adequate tool for solving it, as follows.
Computational intractability. The problem must be suf-
ficiently complex that exact methods (e.g., polynomial-time
optimal solutions to MILP formulations) are infeasible for
real-time operation, hence calling for a heuristic approach.
Evaluation efficiency. The AHD loop shall evaluate thou-
sands of candidate programs hence the problem must allow
for rapid assessment of solution quality. Lightweight, high-
throughput simulation or experimental environments must be
available that accurately capture the performance metrics.
Manageable stochasticity. While networking environments
are inherently stochastic, excessive variance in evaluation
metrics can mask the scoring values of a generated heuristic.
With high noise floor, the evolutionary process may fail to
distinguish between superior logic and lucky random seeds.
B. Atomic Task Selection
Networking functions are often too complex to be addressed
as a whole by AHD. The second fundamental stage of our
approach is the decomposition of the target network function
logic into a fixed skeleton and an evolving atomic task. The
atomic task represents the specific algorithmic logic to be
optimized by the LLM-based AHD. To ensure successful evo-
lution, the atomic task must adhere to the following principles.
Algorithmic logic over parametric tuning. The target must
be the discovery of symbolic logic (i.e., code structure) and not
merely numerical values. If the goal is just optimizing scalar
weights, traditional techniques (e.g., Bayesian optimization)
are more efficient. In contrast, AHD is intended for the
generation of novel algorithmic logic.
Syntactic simplicity, semantic complexity. The atomic
task should be syntactically concise, i.e., short enough to fit
comfortably within the LLM’s context window and minimize
hallucination risks, while governing complex system behav-
iors. A common example in the literature is priority functions:
for instance, in scheduling, a concise mathematical expression
as a priority function that just sorts users or packets, captures
well the complex macroscopic network performance.
Structured state representation. The input to the atomic
task must be structured numerical or categorical data (e.g.,
signal-to-noise ratio, queue depth, latency requirements). Un-
structured inputs, such as raw natural language system logs,
are generally unsuitable for the high-frequency numerical
reasoning required in the context of AHD.


--- Page 3 ---
3
Functional purity. The atomic task should ideally be state-
less and self-contained. It must act as a deterministic mapping
from inputs to outputs without side effects or dependencies on
global variables. This isolation ensures that the evaluation is
robust and that the LLM focuses solely on the transformation
logic rather than state management.
C. Scoring Function Design
The design of the scoring function is the paramount third
stage of our methodology, providing the ground truth that
guides the AHD evolutionary process. In networking contexts,
complex, multi-objective performance metrics shall be con-
densed into a scalar value that reflects all system priorities.
Hierarchical objectives and soft constraints. Networking
problems inherently involve multiple hierarchical objectives.
For instance, satisfying reliability constraints (e.g., outage
probability) should take precedence over service maximization
(e.g., user fairness or throughput). To capture this, the scoring
function must effectively enforce soft constraints. This is
done via linear scalarization or non-linear aggregation. In
linear scalarization, a weighted sum approach approximates
hierarchical preferences; however, this requires careful tuning
of weights across different orders of magnitude to ensure that
violations of primary constraints (e.g., outage) strictly dom-
inate gains in secondary metrics. In non-linear aggregation,
product-based formulations naturally penalize performance
when constraints are violated; here, normalization of input
metrics is critical to prevent numerical instability.
Dense versus sparse feedback. The scoring feedback must
be continuous (dense) rather than binary (sparse). A discrete
function (e.g., returning 0 if an outage occurs, 1 otherwise)
fails to provide the “gradient” necessary for the LLM to
distinguish between a near-miss solution and a catastrophic
failure. A continuous penalty function allows the evolutionary
search to incrementally converge toward feasible regions.
D. Evaluation Methodology
Heuristic evaluation presents unique challenges in the
networking domain compared to pure mathematical discov-
ery. Unlike static combinatorial problems governed solely
by dimensionality, network functions are defined by high-
dimensional, stochastic state spaces including, e.g., topology,
traffic patterns, user mobility, or channel propagation. This
introduces a fundamental trade-off between generalization and
computational cost, as follows.
Generalization versus simulation time. Assessing the
robustness of a heuristic requires averaging performance over a
large distribution of network scenarios and conditions, increas-
ing the evaluation latency per candidate. Excessive simulation
time can bottleneck the evolutionary loop.
Specialization as a feature. Conversely, narrowing the
evaluation to a specific network configuration risks overfitting,
which, can be re-framed as specialization in the context of
AHD. Since generated heuristics are lightweight code snippets,
it is feasible to deploy a library of specialized algorithms tai-
lored to diverse network conditions (e.g., different, dedicated
schedulers for high-mobility and near-static users), rather than
relying on a single “one-size-fits-all” solution.
Fig. 2: 5G LDPC decoding pipeline, highlighting the atomic
task considered for AHD.
III. AHD IN ACTION
We showcase the proposed AHD framework in a repre-
sentative networking use case, i.e., LDPC decoding. We first
present the target task, then describe our AHD implementation
of phases A–D, and finally discuss system settings and results.
A. An Introduction to LDPC Decoding
LDPC allows recovering a transmitted message from a re-
ceived (possibly corrupted) signal. In 5G systems, the receiver
decodes 5G NR Transport Block (TB)s in four conceptual
stages, summarized in Fig. 2.
TB pre-processing. A “soft demapper” produces a Log
Likelihood Ratio (LLR) L(X) per coded bit X, i.e., a real-
valued measure of confidence about bit X ∈{0, 1} given the
channel observation Y . Positive L(X) soft values are inter-
preted as if bit 0 was transmitted, negative values as bit 1, and
values close to zero indicate high uncertainty. Through LLR
Reshaping, the incoming soft values are reorganized so that
they are grouped per TB and per nominal codeword position,
rather than as a flat stream. Descrambling removes the pseudo-
random sequence that was applied at the transmitter, so that
each LLR can again be associated with a specific coded bit.
The Rate Dematching operation conceptually reconstructs the
circular buffer used by 5G NR rate-matching: received bits are
placed back into their buffer positions and neutral padding
bits with zero LLR are inserted wherever no observation
was transmitted, so that the buffer has the correct length.
Channel De-interleaving then reverses the bit-level interleaver
associated with modulation and resource mapping, restoring
the ordering in which bits belong to each TB. Finally, Code
Block Segmentation slices the reconstructed TBs into smaller
LDPC code blocks, each corresponding to one codeword of a
5G NR LDPC base graph.
5G LDPC pre-processing. The above representation is
refined at the level of code blocks (i.e., grouping multiple
bits together), matching an internal LDPC base code. An
Output De-interleaving step reverses the specific 5G rate-
matching interleaver, when configured, arranging the bits in
the exact order required by the LDPC parity-check matrix.
Depuncturing reconstructs punctured parity bits of the TBs in
the standardized 5G NR scheme, either because their structure
is known (header) or because they do not carry additional
information (tail). Shortening Recovery handles the “filler
bits” that were zero-padded before encoding: these bits are
reintroduced with LLRs of very large magnitude, representing


--- Page 4 ---
4
almost infinite confidence that they are equal to the known
filler value. Each LDPC code block now appears to the decoder
as a complete codeword consistent with the 5G NR base graph.
Core LDPC decoding loop. This is an iterative Belief Prop-
agation (BP) loop using a Tanner graph, i.e., a bipartite graph
with “variable nodes” (representing message bits) connected
to “check nodes” (representing parity constraints), and edges
connecting each bit to the checks it participates in. A solution
is valid if every bit satisfies its connected parity check, i.e., the
bits involved in a parity check sum to an even number. Before
decoding starts, LLR Clipping constrains the input values to a
specific range to prevent numerical errors, and a Logit-to-LLR
Conversion ensures that the signs of the internal definition of
L(X) are consistent with the BP update rules.
Each iteration of the core loop then consists of two
main message-passing operations, namely Check Node Up-
date (CNU) and Variable Node Update (VNU), followed by
TB-level checks. In CNU, every check node combines the
incoming variable-to-check messages from all its neighboring
bits and produces outgoing messages back to each of them,
intuitively computing how well the parity equation associated
with that check node is satisfied and pushing each bit towards
those values that make the parity more likely to hold. Different
mathematical rules can be used. We elaborate more on this
in the next subsection, as this is the core of our AHD use
case. In the subsequent VNU, each variable node collects its
incoming check-to-variable messages and combines them with
the original channel LLR for that bit. This results in updated
belief about the bit, expressed as an a-posteriori LLR, and new
outgoing messages to each neighboring check that exclude
the contribution of that specific check (extrinsic information).
Combined, these operations are summarized as Message Con-
struction: channel LLRs and extrinsic information are added
to form the messages that will be used in the next BP iteration.
CRC-based early stopping. A Hard Decision step maps the
a-posteriori LLR soft values into binary “hard” decisions, by
comparing each LLR to zero. From these decoded codewords,
CRC Validation is performed: code-block and transport-block
CRCs are recomputed and their signatures are checked. If
CRC Validation succeeds for a given TB, that TB is marked
as decoded and removed from further iterations. If, after an
iteration, all TBs in the batch have passed CRC Validation, the
outer loop stops; otherwise, the loop continues until either all
TBs are decoded or the maximum iteration budget is reached.
Post-processing. The final information bits are extracted
from the output of the steps above by removing parity bits
and any filler or shortened bits from the decoded TBs.
B. Practical AHD for LDPC Decoding
Our AHD implementation follows the four phases presented
in Sec. II, resulting in the distributed and asynchronous AHD
engine architecture depicted in the bottom part of Fig. 1.
At its core, our AHD tool hinges on the Funsearch model [7]
and is coordinated via shell scripts that automate the startup,
monitoring, and synchronization of all distributed components.
The storage for candidate heuristics is split across several
islands, each maintaining its own collection of code snippets
and their respective scores, organized into clusters. Each
cluster stores a distinct set of programs that share the same
score. Sampler processes constantly communicate with these
islands over HTTP; they request a configurable number of
heuristics (sampled from clusters via a temperature-controlled
scheme that can bias selection toward higher-scoring clusters
as the island grows), augment them with natural-language
instructions and other configurable prompt text, and send them
to an LLM running on one (or more) LLM-server instances.
The model returns a modified version of the same function,
which the sampler forwards to an evaluator.
Evaluators execute candidates in a sandboxed environment,
test them over a fixed set of problem instances, and return
a score. If a candidate produces feasible solutions the corre-
sponding island stores it (placing it into the cluster matching
its score) and updates its local population; if not (e.g., due to
runtime errors, timeouts, or unstable behavior), it is discarded.
In addition to this continuous evolutionary loop, the database
master can periodically trigger a lightweight genetic reset step
across islands, re-seeding weaker islands from stronger ones
according to a configurable reset policy.
All of these components run as separate services, commu-
nicate via simple HTTP APIs, and can be deployed either as
local processes or as Docker containers. We use Qwen3 as the
backbone LLM for code generation and refinement.
A AHD suitability analysis. LDPC decoding is one of the
fundamental, yet most computationally complex, networking
problems. Moreover, it allows for evaluation efficiency, as the
scoring function (later detailed) allows so. Finally, manageable
stochasticity is achieved by considering a small granularity in
the form that context is taken as input.
B Atomic task selection. We aim for algorithmic logic
over parametric tuning. Targeting the whole LDPC processing
may result in larger improvements, but risks to be a too large
task for AHD and curbs linking performance improvements to
individual components. Thus, we focus on a concrete atomic
task, i.e., the CNU function, highlighted in Fig. 2, which
is the most computationally intensive part of the decoding
pipeline. Standard (hand-crafted) functions include Boxplus
(exact sum–product), numerically stabilized Boxplus–ϕ, or ap-
proximations like Min-Sum and Offset Min-Sum. By exploring
alternative implementations beyond these classical baselines,
our goal is to discover new CNU functions that offer a superior
trade-off between reliability and complexity.
The CNU task has a structured state representation and
functional purity: the LDPC decoder can operate under many
combinations of number of physical resource blocks, Mod-
ulation and Coding Scheme (MCS) index, and Signal to
Noise Ratio (SNR) measured in dB. The performance of the
decoding is highly dependent on such state, as shown in Fig. 3
: more complex such “context” entail lower success rates in
correctly decoding messages and more iterations to do so.
C Scoring function design. Our platform aggregates the
outcome of the CNU simulation into a single scalar score
that guides the search. The score yields hierarchical objectives
and soft constraints, as reflects the three main concerns of a
practical decoder: reliability, error-rate quality, and complexity.
Reliability is captured by the number of TBs that remain


--- Page 5 ---
5
Fig. 3: Average number of iterations needed to decode TBs
across network contexts. White indicates that messages cannot
be decoded. The contexts used by our AHD are highlighted, in
black if not all the TBs can be decoded, in purple otherwise.
undecoded after the maximum number of iterations; heuristics
that systematically fail to decode are heavily penalized and
quickly discarded. Among functions that are able to decode
almost all blocks, we then consider the resulting Bit Error
Rate (BER) at the end of the decoding process, so that
small gains in reliability obtained at the cost of much worse
bit-level decisions are not considered attractive. Finally, for
heuristics with comparable reliability and BER, we prefer
those that reach successful decoding in fewer iterations, which
corresponds to lower complexity and latency. The score is
constructed so that these three criteria are effectively ordered
by priority: avoiding pathological behaviour comes first, then
maximizing the number of correctly decoded blocks, then
improving BER, and only then reducing the iteration count.
In practice, this means that the platform behaves as if it was
optimising reliability subject to a minimum quality level, and
only afterwards trading off remaining gains against complex-
ity. We achieve hierarchical objectives and soft constraints by
weighting with different multipliers the above factors in the
score function: 109 for catastrophic failure, 107 for undecoded
TBs count, 106 for the total BER, and 100 for iteration count.
finally, despite the context given as input being naturally
discrete (as apparent in Fig. 3), our score function considers
multiple and dense values where relevant (e.g., for the SNR)
hence allowing for dense versus sparse feedback.
D Evaluation methodology. The score function must be
computed on a choice of target contexts. Fig. 3 reveals three
operating zones: (i) a straightforward region with low itera-
tions and almost no failures towards the bottom right of each
plot; (ii) a hard region with regular decoding failures mapping
to the white region in each plot; and (iii) an intermediate
band where decoding is often successful but requires many
iterations and occasionally fails. We empirically verify that
targeting easy contexts makes the problem undemanding and
brings little incentive for AHD to discover better heuristics,
whereas hard contexts are not informative enough for AHD to
understand the quality of the solutions it generates—as they
tend to yield failures only. Targeting the intermediate band of
contexts with mixed decoding results proves the right choice.
To address the generalization versus simulation time trade-
off, we test different set sizes of contexts (from 30 to 1,
marked by squares in Fig. 3) within the scoring function.
Ultimately, we find that using a single context lying near the
boundary of the region where decoding is possible is sufficient
for AHD: indeed, one context in that area makes the task both
challenging and informative, creating space for exploration of
heuristics that (i) decode more blocks successfully, (ii) reduce
the required iterations, or (iii) improve both metrics at once; at
the same time, extremely poor rules are immediately identified
as they fail on most blocks of the target context. Selecting a
single context also dramatically reduces the memory footprint
of the scoring function and improves the AHD throughput,
making scoring multiple contexts an expensive overkill.
Another dimension of the generalization versus simulation
time trade-off concerns, in the LDPC case, the number of TBs
to be decoded by the generated heuristics. More blocks reduce
statistical noise but increase evaluation costs. Upon experi-
menting, we opt for 30 TBs—fixed to ensure comparability
across AHD generations—in the target single context.
Finally, in the LDPC decoding use case, we do not find
space for specialization as a feature, as using different (mean-
ingful) contexts to score the programs generated by our AHD
implementation yields equivalent heuristics that prove to stay
valid across all other contexts.
C. System-Level Evaluation Settings
We embed and assess all CNU functions in a full link-level
simulation based on Sionna [11] so as to mirror a realistic 5G
NR transport-block processing chain. Starting from random
TBs, the pipeline applies CRC attachment, segmentation into
code blocks, LDPC encoding with 5G NR base graphs, rate
matching, modulation, transmission over a noisy channel, soft
demapping, and finally LDPC decoding with early stopping1
governed by CRC. The candidate CNU function is used
whenever the decoder processes a parity check; all other
operations, including variable-node updates and stopping rules,
are preserved. This guarantees that the evaluation is genuinely
system-level: a successful heuristic must decode TBs reliably
under the same conditions as a conventional decoder, and
coexist with all standard physical-layer procedures.
Finally, it is worth noting that, to address the high latency
of network simulations, our AHD implementation adopts
an asynchronous parallel architecture that utilizes a dis-
tributed pool of evaluators instead of sequential loops. While
LLMs generate candidate programs, multiple independent
workers concurrently simulate different heuristics, maximizing
throughput and ensuring a scalable discovery process.
1Sionna does not natively include an option for early-stopping LDPC 5G
NR decoding, hence we integrated that functionality.


--- Page 6 ---
6
Fig. 4: Results of AHD for LDPC decoding. (a) Evolution of scores versus the number of generated programs, against the Offset
Min-Sum and Boxplus-ϕ baselines. (b) Comparison of decoding performance, with standard deviation across TBs, between the
Boxplus-ϕ algorithm (blue) and the CNU function found by AHD (orange), only for contexts where not all TBs are decoded.
D. Results and Analysis
Fig. 4(a) shows how AHD translates the score design into
decoding performance: as more programs are generated, the
framework produces candidates that first gradually decode
more TBs and later achieve smaller refinements in BER and
needed iterations. This translates into increasing exploration of
higher-score regions (gray areas) and a consequent improve-
ment of the best score (orange). Low-score non-compilable
or numerically unstable proposals still occur throughout the
search as AHD probes new directions, but are discarded by the
evaluator and do not alter the overall trend. The best heuristic
is found after ≈1.3×105 generated programs; when evaluated
on the 30 configuration points of Fig. 3, it decodes all TBs in
the contexts that are fully decodable under Boxplus-ϕ (purple
markers in Fig. 3), and in the harder contexts where not all
TBs can be decoded (black markers in Fig. 3) it is statistically
equivalent to the state-of-the-art Boxplus-ϕ heuristic, as shown
in Fig. 4(b). In a single run, it decoded two additional TBs at
one configuration point, but across 50 independent trials (each
with a different set of TBs) this advantage vanishes, yielding
nearly indistinguishable performance.
The actual code of the CNU function found by AHD is
shown in Fig. 5. It follows the same overall procedure as
the established Boxplus and Boxplus-ϕ, but each of the four
building blocks is realized differently.
In Sign and value separation and management, AHD first
maps incoming LLRs through the tanh nonlinearity and only
then separates sign and magnitude in that tanh domain;
Boxplus-ϕ instead separates sign immediately from the raw
LLRs before any nonlinear mapping, while the tanh-based
Boxplus does not explicitly separate sign and magnitude
because both are implicitly carried inside the signed tanh
values and their product.
In Core function application, AHD stays in the tanh
and atanh formulation but computes the per-check-node
product magnitude via a log-accumulation path (sum of log-
magnitudes followed by exponentiation), whereas Boxplus
forms the same aggregate through a direct product; Boxplus-
ϕ changes domain entirely by mapping magnitudes through
the ϕ function, aggregating by summation, and applying the
ϕ mapping again to return to the LLR domain.
In Intrinsic sign and value removal, AHD excludes the
intrinsic edge by forming a per-node “product of all” quan-
tity and then dividing out the current edge in a controlled
way, while Sionna’s Boxplus relies on an inversion-based
workaround (multiplying by the reciprocal of each edge after
computing the full product), and Boxplus-ϕ performs intrinsic
removal as a sum-excluding-self operation in the ϕ domain.
Finally, in Enforcing numerical stability, AHD concentrates
its hard constraints late in the pipeline and largely at the
aggregated level, stabilizing the per-node product computation
and clamping only at the atanh input boundary; instead,
Boxplus-ϕ enforces stability earlier by clipping inside the
ϕ mapping, which saturates very large magnitudes before
aggregation, and Boxplus introduces additional bookkeeping
steps around near-zero handling to keep direct products and
inversions well-behaved.
IV. CONCLUSION
We adapt and extend LLM-based AHD to the complex
and stochastic domain of network operations. Our four-phase
methodology comprises suitability analysis, atomic function
selection, scoring design, and evaluation, and addresses the
limitations of existing frameworks intended for static combi-
natorial problems. Our implementation of this framework for
5G NR LDPC decoding demonstrates that AHD can discover
novel, interpretable CNU functions with performance on par
with state-of-the-art solutions implemented in production.
Ultimately, this paper makes a first step towards AHD for
network operation, with early results that show the promise
of the approach. Clearly, significant future work is needed to
further tailor AHD to solve complex networking problems, so
as to produce heuristics that substantially outperform current
references in industry-grade target hardware platforms.


--- Page 7 ---
7
Fig. 5: Best CNU function generated by AHD, broken down into its main building blocks.

Agreement No. 101155506 (RIXISAC).
REFERENCES
[1] Y.-H. Jia, Y. Mei, and M. Zhang, “Learning heuristics with different rep-
resentations for stochastic routing,” IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics,
vol. 53, no. 5, pp. 3205–3219, 2023.
[2] C.-Y. Yen, S. Abbasloo, and H. J. Chao, “Computers can learn from
the heuristic designs and master internet congestion control,” in ACM
SIGCOMM ’23.
ACM, 2023, p. 255–274.
[3] A. Agarwal, V. Arun, D. Ray, R. Martins, and S. Seshan, “Towards
provably performant congestion control,” in NSDI 24.
USENIX
Association, Apr. 2024, pp. 951–978.
[4] A. Fox, F. D. Pellegrini, and E. Altman, “Constrained multi-agent
reinforcement learning for task offloading in wireless edge networks,”
in NeurIPS 2025 Workshop: AI and ML for Next-Generation Wireless
Communications and Networking, 2025.
[5] B. Guler, G. Geraci, and H. Jafarkhani, “Robust channel representation
for wireless: A multi-task masked contrastive approach,” in NeurIPS
2025 Workshop: AI and ML for Next-Generation Wireless Communica-
tions and Networking, 2025.
[6] A. Javid and N. G. Prelcic, “Physics-informed neural networks for
wireless channel estimation with limited pilot signals,” in NeurIPS 2025
Workshop: AI and ML for Next-Generation Wireless Communications
and Networking, 2025.
[7] B. Romera-Paredes, M. Barekatain, A. Novikov, M. Balog, M. P. Kumar,
E. Dupont, F. J. R. Ruiz, J. S. Ellenberg, P. Wang, O. Fawzi, P. Kohli,
and A. Fawzi, “Mathematical discoveries from program search with large
language models,” Nature, vol. 625, no. 7995, pp. 468–475, Jan. 2024.
[8] F. Liu, X. Tong, M. Yuan, X. Lin, F. Luo, Z. Wang, Z. Lu, and Q. Zhang,
“Evolution of heuristics: towards efficient automatic algorithm design
using large language model,” ser. ICML’24.
JMLR.org, 2024.
[9] H. Ye, J. Wang, Z. Cao, F. Berto, C. Hua, H. Kim, J. Park, and G. Song,
“Reevo: large language models as hyper-heuristics with reflective evo-
lution,” ser. NIPS ’24, 2024.
[10] F. L. et al., “Llm4ad: Large language model for algorithm design.”
[Online]. Available: https://github.com/Optima-CityU/llm4ad/tree/main/
example/circle packing
[11] J.
Hoydis,
S.
Cammerer,
F.
A.
Aoudia,
A.
Vem,
N.
Binder,
G. Marcus, and A. Keller, “Sionna: An open-source library for
next-generation physical layer research,” 2023. [Online]. Available:
https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.11854
BIOGRAPHIES
Reza Namvar is a PhD student at IMDEA Networks Institute and UC3M.
He holds an MSc in Software Engineering from Shiraz University and a BSc
in Computer Engineering from Azad University.
Jose Gallego is a PhD student at IMDEA Networks Institute and UC3M. He
holds a Bachelor’s in Physics from UCM and a Master’s in Computational
Engineering and Mathematics from URV.
Jose A. Ayala-Romero received his Ph.D. degree from the Technical Uni-
versity of Cartagena, Spain, in 2019. Currently, he is a senior researcher with
the 6G Network group at NEC Laboratories Europe.
Livia Elena Chatzieleftheriou is an MSCA Postdoctoral fellow and a Juan
de la Cierva awardee, currently working with TUDelft. She holds an M.Sc.
in applied mathematics and a Ph.D. in Computer Science.
Andres Garcia-Saavedra is a Principal Research Scientist at NEC Labo-
ratories Europe. He received his Ph.D. from UC3M in 2012 and works on
AI-driven wireless systems, publishing and serving on TPCs for venues such
as ACM MobiCom and IEEE INFOCOM, and journals including IEEE TMC
and IEEE TWC.
Albert Banchs is the Director of IMDEA Networks Institute and Professor
at Universidad Carlos III de Madrid. He earned his PhD (2002) from UPC
BarcelonaTech, and previously worked at Telef´onica and NEC Europe.
Marco Fiore is a Research Professor at IMDEA Networks Institute and co-
founder at Net AI Tech Ltd. He is a Senior Member of IEEE and ACM. His
research at the interface of mobile networks and data science has received
recognitions that include two best paper awards at IEEE INFOCOM.
